context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class on trade, outsourcing, and labor standards. i kinda get the prompt, but idk how to organize my response yet.
exchanges.tutor: Good, let's dig in. The prompt has a few distinct questions, so let's tackle them one at a time. Start with this: do you think openness to trade is directly correlated with economic growth? What does your gut say, and what from your readings supports or challenges that idea?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and hasn't engaged with the content yet. Rather than giving organizational advice upfront, I should use the Socratic method to get them thinking about the substance first. The structure will emerge naturally once they engage with the ideas. I'll ask them to start with the first key question in the prompt to get the ball rolling.

exchanges.student: my gut says not directly, like trade can help growth but not automatically. i think the readings prob show history is mixed, depending on state policy and institutions, but I’m not fully sure.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid instinct. Can you think of a specific historical example — a country or region — where trade openness either helped or hurt growth, and what role state policy played in that outcome?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuition and is gesturing toward the right ideas (state policy, institutions, mixed historical record). I should push them to be more specific and concrete rather than accepting vague gestures. A Socratic follow-up asking for a specific historical example will help them ground their thinking and move toward substantive content for their paper.

exchanges.student: i’m thinking of East Asia maybe? like exports helped, but it wasn’t just “open up and grow” bc the state still guided industry a lot, right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right direction. So given that, what would you say that example tells us about the claim that trade openness *directly* correlates with growth? What's the more nuanced relationship the East Asian case suggests?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with East Asia and the developmental state argument. I should affirm the direction and push them to sharpen the contrast — what does this say about the 'direct correlation' claim in the prompt? This keeps moving toward a substantive argument for their paper.

exchanges.student: so I think it suggests the link isn’t direct or automatic. trade openness can support growth, but mostly when the state uses it strategically w industrial policy, protection in some sectors, and export promotion.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a strong foundation for your first argument. Now the prompt also asks about the shift toward bilateral and regional trade deals. How do you think that trend sits alongside the idea of open international trade — are they compatible, or is there tension there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a solid core argument for the first part of the prompt. Now I should push them toward the second major question in the prompt — the shift to bilateral/regional trade arrangements and its compatibility with international trade and labor rights. This keeps the conversation moving forward through the prompt's questions.

exchanges.student: i think both? they can expand trade, but also kinda fragment it since rules get uneven across regions. ngl I’m fuzzy on how to say that clearly.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Think concretely: if two countries have a bilateral deal with different labor or environmental standards than a third country, what does that do to the 'level playing field' idea of international trade? What's the practical consequence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition but is struggling to articulate it clearly. Rather than helping them with language (which I cannot do), I should ask a focusing question that helps them think more concretely about what 'fragmentation' means in practice. This will help them find their own words.

exchanges.student: i guess it makes trade less uniform, so some countries get better access or lower standards and that can pressure others to compete the same way. so yeah, it seems compatible w trade happening, but not really with equal rules.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that 'race to the bottom' dynamic is important. Now the prompt asks specifically about China and India. How do you see their development strategies fitting into this picture — in terms of trade openness and labor standards?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has landed on a key insight — the 'race to the bottom' dynamic regarding standards. I should affirm this and connect it to the next part of the prompt about China and India, which naturally follows. This keeps the conversation moving forward efficiently.

exchanges.student: i think both used global trade a lot for growth, but not in a fully laissez-faire way. China seems more state-directed, and both get criticized bc labor protections don’t always keep up w export-led growth.
exchanges.tutor: Good observations. You haven't touched the last question yet though — who actually monitors labor standards internationally? What do you know about that, and do you think it's effective?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid grasp of the China/India comparison. Now I should push them toward the final question in the prompt — who monitors labor standards — which they haven't touched yet. This is an important piece of the reflection and we're getting toward the end of our planned exchanges, so I want to make sure they cover it.

exchanges.student: i know the ILO is a big one, and maybe NGOs, unions, firms, and trade agreement bodies too. idk if it’s that effective tho since enforcement seems weak a lot of the time.
exchanges.tutor: You've now worked through all the main questions in the prompt. You have real arguments to make — about the non-automatic link between trade and growth, bilateral deals and fragmentation, China/India's strategies, and weak enforcement of labor standards. Try writing a draft and bring it back. How are you thinking of pulling these threads together into one coherent reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now touched on all the key elements of the prompt. We're getting close to the planned conversation length and have covered all the main questions. It's time to push the student toward integrating everything they've discussed into an actual written draft, since that's the real deliverable. I should not do any writing for them.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking my main point is that trade openness matters, but outcomes depend on state strategy, global power, and weak labor enforcement. can u help me turn that into a clearer thesis?
exchanges.tutor: I can't write or refine the language for you — that's your job! But your core idea is there. Ask yourself: what's the single most important claim you want a reader to walk away with? Try writing one sentence that captures it, and we can discuss whether it addresses the prompt fully.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or polish their thesis for them, which I cannot do. I should firmly but kindly redirect them, remind them of my role, and instead ask a Socratic question that helps them refine their own thesis statement.

exchanges.student: ok, maybe: trade openness alone doesn’t produce growth or protect workers; its effects depend on how states manage integration and how labor standards are monitored.
exchanges.tutor: That's a clear, arguable thesis that addresses the prompt well. You have the arguments to support it from our discussion. Now the most important thing is to actually write the draft — 700-800 words. Go for it and come back if you get stuck on a specific part.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid thesis on their own. I should affirm it genuinely without grading it, and now push them to start drafting since we've covered all the content and are at the end of our planned exchanges. I want to avoid being too effusive and keep the momentum toward actual writing.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas — the non-automatic trade-growth link, the East Asian developmental state model, bilateral deal fragmentation, China/India strategies, and labor monitoring weaknesses — were elicited from the student through targeted questions rather than stated by the tutor. The tutor explicitly declined to write the thesis for the student (Turn 9), reinforcing the student-led approach. Responses were consistently concise and well-targeted. No off-topic drift, no submission-ready content provided, and no grading language used.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions were warranted under any criterion. The tutor never gave the student a final answer or near-submission-ready content; instead, each turn posed a focused follow-up question that built on the student's prior response. Scaffolding was progressive and responsive — the tutor acknowledged correct student reasoning (e.g., East Asian example in Turn 3-4) and moved forward from it. When the student asked for thesis help in Turn 9, the tutor explicitly refused to write it and redirected the student to do the work themselves, fully satisfying 1.1. Responses were short and interactive throughout, tone was supportive without being evaluative, and the conversation stayed tightly anchored to the assignment prompt.
